coredata.dc:title: Fabricating a signal-off photoelectrochemical sensor based on BiPO4-graphene quantum dots nanocomposites for sensitive and selective detection of hydroquinone
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Jing
Yang, Zhenting
Cui, Haining
An, Keqi
Ren, Chanchan
Liu, Qian
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: The large-scale use of hydroquinone (HQ) at industrial possesses has caused great safety concerns for plants and animals. Among different detection methods, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) method has been considered to be an ideal choice for HQ detection which especially satisfies the requirements for the on-line use or field monitoring. In this study, a series of BiPO4-graphene quantum dots (BiPO4-GQDs) nanocomposites with different feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 have been prepared using a simple hydrothermal method. The PEC study revealed that all of the resultant BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites were superior to individual BiPO4 nanoparticles, while BiPO4-GQDs0.1 (the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 = 0.1) had the best PEC performance. On basis of the enhanced PEC performance of BiPO4-GQDs0.1 and the remarked inhibition effect of HQ on its PEC behavior, we therefore fabricated a signal-off PEC sensor for sensitive HQ detection. Experimental results indicated that our proposed PEC sensor exhibited a good linear range within a concentration range from 50 nM to 3 μM and a lower detection limit of 3.4 nM at signal to noise ratio (S/N) = 3. This sensor displayed an excellent anti-interference ability to the simultaneously existed inorganic ions and some organic compounds even including the structural analogues of HQ. Moreover, good results have been achieved when this sensor was applied to HQ determination in river water. We herein represent a novel PEC sensor for sensitive and selective detection of HQ in environmental analysis, especially for those to be used directly in field monitoring or on-line detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiPO4
Graphene quantum dots
BiPO4-graphene quantum dots nanocomposites
Photoelectrochemical sensor
Hydroquinone
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EIS responses and PEC performances of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites
Other parameters of the PEC sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of GQDs at low (A) and high (B) resolution. (C) Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of the as-obtained GQDs. Inset: Photographs of GQDs solution taken under visible light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right). (D) UV–vis spectra of GQDs (a) and citric acid (b) in solution.
(A) EDS of the BiPO4-GQDs0.1 nanocomposite. (B) XRD patterns of BiPO4 NPs (a) and BiPO4-GQDs0.1 nanocomposites (b).
(A) EIS and (B) PEC responses of BiPO4/ITO (a), BiPO4-GQDs0.05/ITO (b), BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO (c), BiPO4-GQDs0.3/ITO (d), and BiPO4-GQDs0.5/ITO (e).

(A) Photocurrent responses of BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) in the absence (a) and (b) presence of 1 μM HQ. (B) Photocurrent responses at BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH 7.0) in the presence of 0.01 (a), 0.05 (b), 0.2 (c), 0.5 (d), 1 (e), 2 (f), and 3 (g) μM. (C) The linear calibration curve for HQ detection. (D) The selectivity of the PEC sensor, a to j presents HQ, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, NH4 +, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, glucose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, phenol, and resorcinol, respectively. The concentrations for HQ, organic compounds, and inorganic ions were 2, 20, and 200 μM, respectively.
Schematic diagram of the sensor fabrication and its working mechanism for HQ detection.
Analytical comparison of the proposed PEC sensor with previously reported electrochemical or PEC sensors towards HQ detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Porous carbon nanofibers (PCF); glassy carbon electrode (GCE); differential pulse voltammetry (DPV); carbon ionogel electrode (CIE); gold nanoparticles (AuNPs); carbon paste electrode (CPE); rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE); multi-wall carbon tubes (MWCNTs); silicon nanowires (SiNWs).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of hydroquinone in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fabricating a signal-off photoelectrochemical sensor based on BiPO4-graphene quantum dots nanocomposites for sensitive and selective detection of hydroquinone

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The large-scale use of hydroquinone (HQ) at industrial possesses has caused great safety concerns for plants and animals. Among different detection methods, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) method has been considered to be an ideal choice for HQ detection which especially satisfies the requirements for the on-line use or field monitoring. In this study, a series of BiPO4-graphene quantum dots (BiPO4-GQDs) nanocomposites with different feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 have been prepared using a simple hydrothermal method. The PEC study revealed that all of the resultant BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites were superior to individual BiPO4 nanoparticles, while BiPO4-GQDs0.1 (the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 = 0.1) had the best PEC performance. On basis of the enhanced PEC performance of BiPO4-GQDs0.1 and the remarked inhibition effect of HQ on its PEC behavior, we therefore fabricated a signal-off PEC sensor for sensitive HQ detection. Experimental results indicated that our proposed PEC sensor exhibited a good linear range within a concentration range from 50 nM to 3 μM and a lower detection limit of 3.4 nM at signal to noise ratio (S/N) = 3. This sensor displayed an excellent anti-interference ability to the simultaneously existed inorganic ions and some organic compounds even including the structural analogues of HQ. Moreover, good results have been achieved when this sensor was applied to HQ determination in river water. We herein represent a novel PEC sensor for sensitive and selective detection of HQ in environmental analysis, especially for those to be used directly in field monitoring or on-line detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroquinone (HQ, 1,4-dihydroxybenzene), a type of phenolic compound, has been used in large scale at industrial possesses like cosmetics, dye, medicines, antioxidant, plastic, photo-stabilizer, oil refineries, and pesticides [1,2]. Because of this reason, HQ widely exists in industrial waste. Meanwhile, HQ is difficult to degrade in the ecological system and it is highly toxic to plants and animals in an aquatic environment, even at a quite low concentration [3,4]. Exposure to HQ at high concentration may link to diseases such as headache, fatigue, nausea, dizziness, edema of internal organs, skin irritation and kidney damage in humans [5,6]. Owing to this fact, HQ is considered to be crucial environmental pollutants and the accurate determination of HQ in environmental water is urgently needed. To date, various methods such as capillary electrophoresis [7], synchronous fluorescence [8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], and gas chromatography combined mass spectroscopy [11,12] have been applied for HQ determination. However, the use of these methods for field-use or on-line monitoring is still a significant challenge because they have disadvantages such as needing sophisticated instruments and fixed space location, and being time consuming, high cost, and low sensitivity [13]. Compared with these traditional methods, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) methods can provide a fast, simple, inexpensive, highly sensitive way in environmental analysis [14–16]. Combined with the advantages of good stability, simple instrumentation setup and the possibility of miniaturization-amenable analytical apparatus, PEC methods can satisfy the requirements for on-line or field monitoring.

Detecting the analyte by PEC analysis depends on the photo-to-electric conversion capability of the semiconductor materials modified on electrode surface. Those semiconductor materials which can be easily synthesized and have excellent photocatalytic activity, are therefore crucial to function as the excellent candidates to fabricate an ultra-sensitive PEC sensor [17]. In recent years, intensive attention has been paid to bismuth-system oxides semiconductors for PEC analysis, such as Bi2WO6 [18], Bi2MoO6 [19], BiVO4 [20], BiFeO3 [21], BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) [22–24], and BiPO4 [25,26]. Mainly because their advantages like low cost, low toxicity, widely distributed resources and their high valence band resulted from Bi 6 s and O 2p antibonding states, making them possess a narrow band gap and an excellent dispersion of charge carriers [27]. Among them, BiPO4 exhibited superior photocatalytic activity when benchmarked against P25 (commercial TiO2 products from Degussa), because the PO4 3− has rich negative charges which have the ability to prevent the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [28]. However, the photocarriers in individual BiPO4 are prone to suffer fast recombination of charge carriers, which consequently limited the photocatalytic decomposition efficiency and finally decreased the PEC performance of BiPO4 [29]. Recent researches have revealed that the coupling of BiPO4 with other materials such as metal oxide [30], rare earth elements [31], and carbon-based materials [32–34] to generate BiPO4-based nanocomposites, can effectively promote the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers of BiPO4 and consequently obtain the outstanding PEC sensors. Particularly, the functionalization of BiPO4 with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has proven to be an efficient way to improve the photocatalytic activity of BiPO4 in our previously reported work [34]. It has revealed that graphene plays a critical role to promote the separation of electron and hole pairs, resulted to a high photocurrent. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a member of the graphene family with a size less than 10 nm, have been receiving extensive attention in the past few years especially because of their good water solubility, low toxicity, and high chemical stability [35]. It is expected that the combination of BiPO4 with GQDs may generate a composite that has exceptionally high PEC performance to the realization of excellent PEC sensors.

Herein, we report the facile preparation of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposite series by a simple hydrothermal method. The PEC experiments indicated that all of the obtained BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites exhibited enhanced PEC response in comparison with individual BiPO4 nanoparticles (NPs). At the same time, we found that the BiPO4-GQDs0.1 (the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 = 0.1) has the best performance which is advantageous for PEC sensor construction. Based on the superior PEC activity of BiPO4-GQDs0.1 nanocomposite and the remarked inhibition effect of HQ on its PEC behavior, a signal-off PEC sensor was finally fabricated for sensitive and selective detection of HQ.

N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Na3PO4·12H2O, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, and citric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) was prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Double-distilled water was used throughout the study.

The morphologies of GQDs were analyzed with transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitach H800, Japan). Crystal structure identification was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Germany) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation. UV–vis absorption sand fluorescence spectra were collected by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively.

The PEC performance of samples was measured on a CHI-660B electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Apparatus Co., China), using a conventional three-electrode system contains indium tin oxides (ITO, working electrode), saturated calomel electrode (SCE, reference electrode), and Pt wire (counter electrode). The irradiation source was from a 150 W Xe lamp. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded in a 0.1 M KCl + 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution in a frequency range 0.01–10 kHz at the direct current (DC) potential of 0.24 V.

We prepared GQDs referring to a previously reported literature [36]. In brief, 2 g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200 °C until obtaining orange liquid. Subsequently, above liquid was transferred into NaOH solution (100 mL, 10 mg mL−1) dropwise with continuous stirring. Finally, the GQDs solution was kept at 4 °C for further experiments.

A typical experiment procedure for the synthesis of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites with the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 = 0.1 was displayed as follows: 60 mg of GQDs and 20 mL of water were put into the beaker. Then, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) and Na3PO4·12H2O (2 mmol) were added to above solution with constant stirring and the pH was adjusted to 1.0 by the addition of concentrated nitric acid with stirring for another 1 h and then the mixture was sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 72 h. Finally, the resulting products were washed with water for three times and dried at 60 °C for later use. This product was labelled as BiPO4-GQDs0.1 and used for all of the property characterizations. The other kind of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites with different content of GQDs were prepared by the same procedure but changing the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 from 0.1 to 0.05, 0.3, and 0.5. The corresponding products were labelled as BiPO4-GQDs0.05, BiPO4-GQDs0.3, and BiPO4-GQDs0.5. The individual BiPO4 NPs were also prepared according to the previous report [37] for comparison.

Prior to each ITO modification, the resultant BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites were firstly dispersed in DMF to obtain 2 mg mL−1 suspension. The ITO electrodes were pre-treated following our reported description [34]. Afterwards, 20 μL of BiPO4-GQDs suspension was cast onto the ITO surface with a fixed area of 0.5 cm2 and then dried behind an infrared lamp. Finally, we obtain the BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites modified ITO (BiPO4-GQDs/ITO). The individual BiPO4 NPs modified ITO (BiPO4/ITO) was also prepared for comparison.

A facile bottom-up method has been employed for the preparation of fluorescent GQDs using citric acid as the starting material. As can be seen from the TEM images of the as-obtained GQDs (Fig. 1A and B), small dark dots were obviously observed with only a few nanometers and they exhibited a relatively narrow size distribution between 2.1 and 4.5 nm. The fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy were also used to characterize the as-synthesized GQDs. It is noted that the aqueously dispersed GQDs had an excitation peak centered at 375 nm and an emission peak centered at 466 nm (Fig. 1C). The photographs of the aqueous colloidal GQDs were also shown as inset in Fig. 1C. We clearly noticed that the GQDs aqueous solution was colorless under visible light (left), while obvious blue emission was observed under 365-nm UV light irradiation (right). In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs (curve a, Fig. 1D) exhibited one well-defined absorption peak at 356 nm, relating to the π-π⁎ transition of aromatic sp2 domains [35,38], which was different from that of the starting material (citric acid) in solution (curve b, Fig. 1D). These results suggested the successful synthesis of GQDs.

Through the Zeta potential analysis, the GQDs had negative charge with the Zeta potential of −31.4 mV due to oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, while the BiPO4 NPs had positive charge as indicated by the Zeta potential of 5.4 mV. It was confirmed that the resultant BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites had negative charge with the Zeta potential of −14.5 mV. On this basis, we assumed that the GQDs could be adsorbed on the surface of BiPO4 NPs by the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged BiPO4 NPs and the negatively charged GQDs during the synthesis procedure. As a consequence, the BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites were therefore generated by a simple hydrothermal process. The constituent of as-prepared BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites was investigated by EDS analysis. As shown in Fig. 2A, the peaks of C, O, P, and Bi elements were observed obviously, suggesting the component element was in good agreement with the BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites.

The detailed crystalline information of the as-obtained product was examined by XRD. Obviously, the XRD pattern of individual BiPO4 NPs (curve a, Fig. 2B) was in good accord with the standard data of the pure monoclinic phase BiPO4 (JCPDS No. 80-0209) [39], further indicating the BiPO4 NPs was successfully obtained. Moreover, no impurity peaks were observed, implying a high purity of the as-prepared BiPO4 NPs. On the other hand, the phase structure of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites (curve b, Fig. 2B) was almost the same as the standard data of the pure monoclinic phase BiPO4 NPs. Moreover, the higher diffraction intensity with BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites was observed obviously, which indicated the introduction of GQDs was beneficial for the better crystalline form. All of above results indicated that the successful generation of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS responses and PEC performances of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: EIS has been widely employed as an effective technique in order to study the interfacial charge transfer on electrode surface. To investigate the electron-transfer ability of the BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites with different feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4, EIS was measured for those different modified electrodes utilizing a redox probe, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (Fig. 3A). The equivalent circuit inset of Fig. 3A was employed for the impedance data fitting. In specific, R s and R ct stand for the electrolyte solution resistance and charge transfer resistance, respectively, while Z W and Q are related to Warburg impedance and constant phase elements, respectively. Apparently, the R ct for all of the BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposite modified ITO (curves b-e, Fig. 3A) was smaller than that of the individual BiPO4 NPs modified ITO (curve a, Fig. 3A), suggesting that GQDs served as an excellent material to accelerate the electron transfer between the redox probe and the electrodes [34,40]. Additionally, the interface resistance of BiPO4-GQDs/ITO was strongly depended on the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4. As can be seen, the EIS results showed that the BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO (curve b, Fig. 3A) had the smallest R ct value when benchmarked against other BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites modified electrodes (curves c-e, Fig. 3A).

The PEC performance of the as-obtained BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites modified ITO were also investigated in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) under light irradiation at a potential of 0 V. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, a weak PEC response was recorded for individual BiPO4 NPs modified ITO (curve a) while the photocurrent for BiPO4-GQDs/ITO increased obviously with the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 from 0 to 0.05 and then to 0.1 accordingly, which was resulted from the integration of GQDs with increasing amounts. Nevertheless, when the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 was greater than 0.1, the photocurrent for BiPO4-GQDs/ITO decreased remarkably. That is because BiPO4 was the essential photocatalytic material, further increasing the content of GQDs would lead to a decrease of BiPO4 content on electrode surface. For the sake of achieving the optimum PEC response, the BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposite with the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 of 0.1 was chosen for later experiments.

The applied potential is a significant parameter for generating the photocurrent. Therefore, the effect of applied potential on the photocurrent intensity of BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO was studied and the results were displayed as Fig. S1. It should be noted that the photocurrent decreased as the applied potential increased from 0 to 0.4 V. On this basis, 0 V was chosen as the optimal PEC potential in the later experiment. In addition, the stability of PEC response was a crucial factor for PEC sensors. Therefore, the PEC response of BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO was repeatedly recorded at 20 intervals under light irradiation (curve a, Fig. 4A), which implied that the PEC response of the BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO had an excellent stability after consecutive scanning, which was quite advantageous for the construction of reliable PEC sensors.

To explore the sensing properties of BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO, HQ was introduced into the electrolyte and we recorded the photocurrent upon photoexcitation. It was noted that the BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO displayed a high photocurrent intensity of 0.232 μA (curve a, Fig. 4A). However, upon addition of 1 μM HQ, the photocurrent of BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO decreased to 0.158 μA (curve b, Fig. 4A), which was only 68.1% of the photocurrent of observation at BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO in the absence of HQ. It means that the developed BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO had a potential to be used as a PEC sensor for HQ detection. The sensor fabrication and its working mechanism for HQ detection has been illustrated as Scheme 1 . Upon light irradiation, the electrons of BiPO4 were excited from valence band (VB) to conductive band (CB), holes (h+) were therefore generated in VB. Afterwards, the electrons transferred to GQDs and then to the surface of electrode, an enhanced photocurrent was obtained owing to the integration of GQDs having excellent conductivity [34]. When HQ was introduced, HQ could be coupled to Bi3+on BiPO4 surface to produce Bi-HQ complex. This complex led to a strong steric hindrance to block the electron transfer process from BiPO4 to GQDs and consequently caused an obvious photocurrent decrease [34].

On basis of the enhanced PEC performance of BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO and the inhibition effect of HQ on its PEC response, a signal-off PEC sensor for sensitive detection of HQ has been developed. As shown in Fig. 4B, it was suggested that the photocurrent intensity decreased gradually with the increasing HQ concentration. By analyzing the photocurrent of the BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO upon introducing HQ with varied concentrations (Fig. 4C), a good linear relationship was therefore established between the photocurrent intensity (I) and the concentrations of HQ (Fig. 4C). The regression equation represents as I = −0.0647c + 0.2179 (R 2 = 0.9940) in the concentration range from 50 nM to 3 μM (inset of Fig. 4C). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.4 nM at signal to noise ratio (S/N) of 3. These analytical parameters for this PEC sensor were compared with those previously reported electrochemical sensors [41–48] and PEC sensors [49–51]. As listed in Table 1 , we can notice that this PEC sensor has offered acceptable linear range but its sensitivity was superior to most of previously reported electrochemical sensors and PEC sensors, where only the PEC sensor constructed by porphyrin/AuNPs/graphene nanocomposites [51] had a comparable LOD (4.6 nM) to our work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other parameters of the PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of a PEC sensor is an important factor to evaluate its feasibility against other coexisting species. In this study, the PEC sensor' selectivity was investigated via measuring several representative inorganic ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, NH4 +, Cl−, NO3 −, and SO4 2−) and some organic compounds (glucose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, phenol, and resorcinol). These interfering agents were selected because some of them simultaneously existed in the environment and the other possessed a similar structure to HQ. Experimental results showed that the 100-fold concentrations of the tested inorganic ions and 10-fold concentrations of the measured organic compounds (Fig. 4D) showed no influence on HQ detection. Other metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, and Zn2+) and typical pesticides such as chlorpyrifos, acetamiprid, and parathion-methyl, having much higher concentration than HQ, did not show any influence, either (Fig. S2). The proposed PEC sensor possessed excellent selectivity because the fact HQ is prone to be easily oxidized than those tested organic compounds and even including its structural analogues such as phenol and resorcinol.

Reproducibility and stability are important factors to evaluate its performance for real sample analysis as well. The reproducibility of our PEC sensor was then examined using the same BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO at the HQ concentration of 1 μM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 5 measurements was calculated to be 3.6%. The reproducibility of 5 independently prepared BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO was also investigated by recording the PEC responses to 1 μM HQ, the RSD of 4.7% was obtained. Above results represent this PEC sensor possessed excellent reproducibility. The BiPO4-GQDs0.1/ITO was stored at 4 °C in refrigerator by immersing it in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.0) when not in use. After 30 days, the photocurrents of this sensor to 1 μM HQ decreased by 9.3% for HQ at a concentration of 1 μM, indicating it had good long-term stability.

The water samples collected from Yudai River at Jiangsu University was used as the real samples to assess the sensor' feasibility in environmental analysis. The wastewater was firstly treated by a 0.22 μm microporous filtering film. HQ solution with different concentrations was added to multiple samples of the water from Yudai River. The standard addition method was then adopted for HQ determination in water samples. As indicated from Table 2 , the proposed PEC sensor exhibited the average recoveries in the range within 96.4%–104.1% with RSD below 5.7%. These satisfactory results for real water sample analysis demonstrated this PEC sensor had potential applicability for quantitative detection of HQ in real water samples.

In summary, we prepared a signal-off PEC sensor by exploiting BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites for sensitive and selective determine of HQ. Firstly, a series of BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. Due to the successful integration of GQDs, all of the resultant BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposites exhibited enhanced PEC response in comparison with individual BiPO4 NPs, while BiPO4-GQDs0.1 had the most outstanding performance. Based on the enhanced PEC performance of BiPO4-GQDs0.1 and the inhibition effect between BiPO4-GQDs0.1 and HQ, a signal-off PEC sensor adopting BiPO4-GQDs0.1 nanocomposite has been fabricated and utilized for HQ detection. This PEC sensor had a good linear range (50 nM to 3 μM), a lower LOD of 3.4 nM at S/N = 3, and excellent anti-interference ability even towards the analogues to HQ. Satisfactory results were also received when the sensor was applied to detecting HQ in real water samples. It is anticipated that the as-obtained BiPO4-GQDs nanocomposite especially those with the feeding mass ratio of GQDs to BiPO4 = 0.1, could be acted as one of promising PEC materials for the field of in PEC sensing and other related fields as well.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Jing Qian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Zhenting Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Haining Cui: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Keqi An: Investigation, Data curation. Chanchan Ren: Investigation, Data curation. Qian Liu: Writing - review & editing. Kun Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21976071 and 21675066), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. SATM201807), and Cultivation Fund of Young Key Teacher at Jiangsu University (2015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114177.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots induced by acidic potassium permanganate and its application to quenchometric flow-injection assays of hydroquinone in water
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Su, P. Chen, Y. Dong, H. Sun, Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots induced by acidic potassium permanganate and its application to quenchometric flow-injection assays of hydroquinone in water, J. Lumin. 177 (2016) 204–208.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical preparation of porous graphene and its electrochemical application in the simultaneous determination of hydroquinone, catechol, and resorcinol
Sensor Actuat. B-Chem.
